text: Herd behavior in financial markets has a precise mechanism: information cascades. Economist Sushil Bikhchandani showed that a rational individual, observing others act, may rationally discard their own private information and follow the herd — because the herd's revealed preferences constitute stronger evidence than one's own signal. This is not irrationality; it is Bayesian updating taken to a pathological extreme. The same logic governs trending topics: each retweet signals others that this content is worth attending to, creating a cascade in which aggregate attention becomes its own justification. The crowd is self-validating by design.
question: In Bikhchandani's model of information cascades, why might a rational person follow the herd against their own private information?

options.text: Social pressure overcomes their capacity for independent reasoning
The herd's collective revealed preferences constitute stronger evidence than one's own signal
Individual information is always less accurate than group information
Bayesian updating requires discarding prior beliefs entirely
